title: Measure Growth in Compact Simple Lie Groups
speaker: Yifan Jing
abstract: The celebrated product theorem says if A is a generating subset of a finite simple group of Lie type G, then |AAA| \gg \min \{ |A|^{1+c}, |G| \}. In this talk, I will show that a similar phenomenon appears in the continuous setting: If A is a subset of a compact simple Lie group G, then \mu(AAA) greater than \min \{ (3+c)\mu(A), 1 \}, where \mu is the normalized Haar measure on G. I will also talk about how to use this result to solve the Kemperman Inverse Problem, and discuss what will happen when G has high dimension or when G is non-compact.

proof_stages.label: Small product growth and tripling heuristic
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from abelian sumset and measure-growth models, then uses finite simple group product theorems and the subgroup-plus-point obstruction to argue that genuinely nonabelian groups should expand under tripling rather than merely doubling.

proof_stages.label: Approximate-group structure background
proof_stages.summary: Approximate groups are defined via covering of X^2 by few translates of X, related to small tripling, and placed in context through BGT-type finite structure theorems and locally compact analogues, with emphasis on the continuous local-Lie obstruction and the lack of effective quantitative bounds.

proof_stages.label: Quantitative compact simple Lie group expansion
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states quantitative Haar-measure growth results for compact simple Lie groups, explains why uniform twofold-product expansion can fail, and motivates sharper SO(3)-type conjectures and the need to understand global shape.

proof_stages.label: Kemperman inverse and toric alternative
proof_stages.summary: The compact growth result is positioned as an input to inverse and near-equality theorems for Kemperman-type inequalities, leading to the dichotomy that a connected compact group either expands or admits a torus quotient accounting for equality.

proof_stages.label: High-dimensional and noncompact variants
proof_stages.summary: Related results are described for high-dimensional compact simple Lie groups and for connected noncompact unimodular groups, where product-measure inequalities involve the noncompact dimension and an exceptional h-parameter discussed further in questions.

proof_stages.label: Almost-linear pseudometric reduction
proof_stages.summary: The proof strategy is to associate to near equality a pseudometric, modeled on the linear metric of a torus and concretely given by symmetric differences of translates of A, so that linearity would force a torus quotient by the kernel.

proof_stages.label: Nonconcentration and good one-dimensional fibers
proof_stages.summary: A key nonconcentration proposition rules out Kakeya-like concentration of A along every one-dimensional torus, allowing the choice of a torus direction H whose coset fibers are short and can be used to compare the pseudometric with fiber distances.

proof_stages.label: Random-fiber Kemperman step and no-small-subgroups closure
proof_stages.summary: Randomly sampling fibers and applying the torus Kemperman inequality forces near-equality fibers to look like intervals, while the Lie-group no-small-subgroups property prevents inconsistent behavior under further translates and completes the structural argument.

examples.name: Small exponential ball in a Lie group
examples.note: The image of a sufficiently small Lie-algebra neighborhood under the exponential map is an approximate group even in a far-from-nilpotent Lie group, showing why the finite BGT conclusion cannot transfer naively to open approximate groups.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker constructs exp(lambda B) from a small Lie-algebra ball and checks it is an approximate group because multiplication is locally additive
